subject: Request for Comment: FOMC September 2023 Rate Decision and Forward Guidance
body: I am a financial reporter with the Wall Street Journal covering the Federal Reserve's September 2023 FOMC meeting outcome. The committee raised the federal funds rate by 25 basis points to a target range of 5.25–5.50%, and I am seeking an official comment on the Board's rationale for maintaining the possibility of additional hikes in the dot plot projections. Specifically, I would like clarification on how recent core PCE data influenced the decision and whether Chair Powell's remarks signal a prolonged restrictive stance. Could a FRBSF spokesperson be made available for a brief on-record comment by end of business Thursday?

subject: Why are interest rates so high? I can't afford my mortgage anymore
body: I just want to understand why the Federal Reserve keeps raising interest rates when regular people like me are struggling to pay our bills. My adjustable-rate mortgage payment went up almost $600 a month this year and I feel like nobody at the Fed cares about working families. I heard on the news that you are trying to fight inflation but my groceries are still expensive AND my mortgage is through the roof. Is there any plan to lower rates soon or are we just supposed to keep suffering?

subject: Inquiry Regarding Proposed Changes to Basel III Endgame Capital Requirements — Community Bank Impact
body: On behalf of Pacific Coast Community Bancorp, we write to formally request clarification on the Federal Reserve's July 2023 proposed rulemaking regarding the Basel III Endgame capital framework and its potential implications for mid-sized and community banking institutions with assets between $10 billion and $100 billion. Our preliminary analysis suggests the standardized approach for credit risk-weighted assets could increase our required capital buffer by approximately 12–18%, which would materially constrain our capacity to extend small business and agricultural lending across California's Central Valley. We respectfully request a meeting with FRBSF supervisory staff to discuss the comment letter submission process and explore whether tailoring provisions might be extended to institutions of our size. We look forward to your guidance before the January 2024 comment deadline.

subject: Question about Federal Reserve's 2% inflation target and whether it will change
body: I called because I read an article saying some economists want the Fed to raise its inflation target from 2% to 3% and I wanted to know if that is actually being considered. I am retired and living on a fixed income, and even 2% inflation every year eats into my savings pretty significantly over time. I would appreciate knowing whether the San Francisco Fed or the FOMC has any official position on changing the target, and where I could find reliable information about this topic.

subject: Congressional Staff Briefing Request: Labor Market Conditions in the 12th Federal Reserve District
body: This inquiry is submitted on behalf of the Office of Senator Maria Cantwell, Senate Committee on Commerce, Science, and Transportation. We are preparing for upcoming hearings on labor market disparities in the Western United States and wish to request a staff-level briefing with FRBSF economists regarding the Bank's research on employment outcomes across demographic groups in the 12th District, particularly in light of the November 2024 jobs report showing nonfarm payroll gains moderating to 180,000. We are especially interested in the FRBSF's analysis of wage growth sustainability given current monetary policy settings and the dual mandate balance between maximum employment and price stability. Please advise on available dates in late January or early February 2025 for a virtual or in-person briefing.

subject: Press Inquiry: FRBSF President Comments on January 2023 FOMC Decision
body: I am a financial reporter with the Wall Street Journal covering the Federal Reserve's January 31-February 1, 2023 FOMC meeting and the anticipated 25 basis point rate hike. I would like to request a comment or on-record statement from FRBSF President Mary Daly regarding the pace of future rate increases and whether the Fed believes it has done enough to bring inflation back to the 2% target. Could you also confirm whether President Daly will be available for a brief media availability following the meeting announcement? My deadline for this story is February 2, 2023 at 5:00 PM Eastern.

subject: Why does raising interest rates hurt regular people like me?
body: I don't really understand why the Federal Reserve keeps raising interest rates when it's making everything harder for regular working people. My mortgage payment went up and my credit card interest is through the roof. I thought the Fed was supposed to help the economy, not make it worse for everyday Americans trying to get by. Can someone please explain in plain English why this is happening and when it will stop?

subject: Request for Clarification on FRBSF Supervisory Guidance Regarding CRE Loan Concentration Limits
body: On behalf of Pacific Coast Community Bank, we are writing to seek clarification on the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco's current supervisory expectations related to commercial real estate loan concentration risk, particularly in light of recent guidance following the March 2023 banking stress events. Our institution has observed divergent interpretations of the applicable concentration thresholds among peer institutions in the Twelfth Federal Reserve District, and we believe uniform guidance would greatly benefit our compliance planning. We respectfully request a meeting with FRBSF supervisory staff or written clarification of applicable standards at your earliest convenience. Please direct your response to our Chief Risk Officer, details enclosed.

subject: Concern About Job Market and Fed Policy Impact on Small Businesses
body: I called to express my concern about how the Federal Reserve's tight monetary policy is affecting small business owners like myself in the San Francisco Bay Area. I run a small catering company and the cost of borrowing to cover payroll gaps has become almost unmanageable with rates where they are now. I heard that the Fed's dual mandate includes maximum employment, so I'm wondering why officials seem willing to risk layoffs just to fight inflation numbers. I'd appreciate any information on how the Fed balances these priorities and whether relief is coming anytime soon.

subject: Congressional Inquiry: Constituent Concerns Regarding FRBSF QT Program and Treasury Market Liquidity
body: This office is writing on behalf of Congresswoman Linda Chávez of California's 14th Congressional District to request information regarding the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco's perspective on the ongoing quantitative tightening program and its potential effects on Treasury market liquidity conditions. Several constituent financial institutions and investment advisors have raised concerns that the accelerated runoff of the Fed's balance sheet, now approaching $1 trillion in reductions from its peak, may be contributing to episodic volatility in short-term funding markets. We respectfully ask for a briefing or written response addressing these concerns and the FRBSF's assessment of systemic risk implications. Please respond to our district office liaison no later than 30 days from the date of this correspondence.

subject: Request for Comment: FRBSF President's Remarks on Labor Market Tightness
body: My name is Priya Anand and I'm a financial correspondent with Reuters covering the Federal Reserve beat. Following Mary Daly's comments at the recent Brookings Institution event regarding the labor market's resilience in the face of rate hikes, I'd like to request an on-record response or interview opportunity to discuss the FRBSF's current assessment of wage growth and its implications for the dual mandate. Specifically, I'm interested in whether the bank views current unemployment levels as consistent with a soft landing scenario. Could you please confirm availability for a press call before our publication deadline of Friday, March 15th?

subject: Why is my mortgage rate so high if inflation is coming down?
body: I've been watching the news and they keep saying inflation is dropping and getting closer to your 2% target, but my bank just quoted me 7.1% on a 30-year fixed mortgage. I thought when inflation goes down the Fed would lower rates and mortgages would get cheaper. Can someone explain why everyday people like me are still getting punished with high rates even though the data seems to be improving? I'm trying to buy my first home and this is really frustrating.

subject: Formal Inquiry Regarding Proposed Basel III Endgame Capital Requirements and Community Bank Impact
body: On behalf of the Western Independent Bankers Association, we are writing to formally request clarification and engagement from the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco regarding the proposed Basel III endgame rules published in the Federal Register in July 2023. Our member institutions, predominantly community and regional banks with assets under $100 billion, are deeply concerned that certain elements of the expanded risk-based capital framework may disproportionately constrain lending capacity in underserved rural and agricultural markets across the Twelfth Federal Reserve District. We respectfully request a meeting with FRBSF supervision staff and the opportunity to submit supplemental economic impact data prior to the close of the comment period. We believe a tailored approach consistent with the spirit of the Economic Growth, Regulatory Relief, and Consumer Protection Act remains essential for preserving community bank viability.

subject: Question about Federal Reserve balance sheet reduction and its effect on retirement savings
body: I'm a retired school teacher living on a fixed income and I've been hearing a lot about the Fed shrinking its balance sheet through something called quantitative tightening. I'm worried about what this means for my bond mutual funds inside my IRA, because my financial advisor mentioned that bond prices drop when yields go up. Is there somewhere on your website or a publication you can send me that explains in plain language what QT means for ordinary savers and when it might end?

subject: Congressional Inquiry: FRBSF Economic Data on Regional Employment Recovery Post-COVID
body: This inquiry is submitted on behalf of Congressman Rafael Torres, ranking member of the House Financial Services Subcommittee on Monetary Policy and Treasury Market Robustness. The Congressman's office is preparing materials for an upcoming oversight hearing on the effectiveness of Federal Reserve monetary policy actions taken between 2020 and 2024 in restoring full employment across geographically diverse regions, particularly within the Twelfth District. We are requesting access to any published or forthcoming FRBSF Economic Letter analyses, as well as any staff-level briefing availability, covering sectoral employment recovery disparities in California, Nevada, and Arizona. Please respond to this office no later than ten business days prior to the scheduled hearing date of May 22, 2024.

subject: Request for Comment: FRBSF Economic Letter on Labor Market Slack and Wage Inflation
body: My name is Priya Venkataraman and I am a senior economics correspondent at the Financial Times. We are publishing a piece this Friday examining the FRBSF's recent Economic Letter suggesting residual labor market slack may be dampening wage-price pass-through more than headline unemployment figures indicate. I would like to request a brief statement from a spokesperson or the authoring economist on how this research informs the San Francisco Fed's current read on the Phillips curve. Could you also confirm whether President Daly will be available for a short interview ahead of the next FOMC blackout period?

subject: Why does the Fed keep raising rates when regular people can't afford their mortgages?
body: I just want to understand why the Federal Reserve keeps hiking interest rates when people like me are getting priced out of refinancing our homes. My adjustable-rate mortgage payment went up almost $400 a month since last year and I don't know how long I can keep up. I read that the Fed's goal is 2% inflation but prices at the grocery store are still really high — so is it even working? Please explain what the Fed is actually doing to help working families and not just Wall Street.

subject: Inquiry Regarding Proposed Updates to Regulation YY Enhanced Prudential Standards — Comment Period
body: On behalf of Pacific Coast Community Bancorp, I am writing to formally request clarification on the Federal Reserve Board's proposed revisions to Regulation YY Enhanced Prudential Standards as outlined in the October 2023 NPR. Specifically, we seek guidance on how the revised liquidity coverage ratio thresholds will apply to Category IV institutions with total consolidated assets between $100 billion and $250 billion, particularly in light of the supervisory lessons drawn from the March 2023 bank failures. Our compliance team has identified potential ambiguities in the proposed calculation methodology for net stable funding ratios that we believe warrant additional Federal Reserve guidance before the comment period closes on January 16, 2024. We respectfully request a meeting with supervisory staff at the FRBSF to discuss these concerns in detail.

subject: Question about the Fed's balance sheet reduction and what quantitative tightening means for savings accounts
body: Hi, I called because I've been hearing a lot about the Fed shrinking its balance sheet and quantitative tightening and I honestly have no idea what that means for someone like me with a regular savings account. I saved up about $15,000 and I want to know if this QT thing is good or bad for the interest rate I'm earning. The person at my bank told me rates might come down soon but I saw news saying the Fed might hold rates higher for longer — I'm confused about who to believe.

subject: Congressional Inquiry — FRBSF Stress Testing Methodology for Regional Banks Post-SVB
body: This inquiry is submitted on behalf of Congressman Alan Firth, member of the House Committee on Financial Services, regarding the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco's supervisory and stress testing practices as applied to Silicon Valley Bank prior to its failure in March 2023. The Congressman's office is preparing for upcoming committee hearings and requests all non-privileged documentation related to FRBSF examination findings and internal escalation procedures for SVB covering the period January 2022 through March 2023. We additionally request a briefing with FRBSF supervisory leadership no later than 30 days from the date of this communication, consistent with Congressional oversight authority. Please confirm receipt and provide a point of contact for coordination.
sender_organization: Office of Congressman Alan Firth, U.S. House of Representatives
